coredata.dc:title: A dual-channel luminescent signal readout nanoprobe for rapid monitoring of biogenic amines in milk and yogurt
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Genping
Zhang, Caizhong
Du, Peiyao
Sun, Shihao
Zhang, Xuan
Wang, Baodui
Lu, Xiaoquan
coredata.dc:description: As biologically active small molecular, biogenic amines (BAs) could be served as an important index of food spoilage and contamination. In addition, excessive intake of biogenic amines usually can cause a serious impact on human health. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a reliable, sensitive, and non-invasive method for the detection of biogenic amines in foods. To address the above problems, optical sensing method utilizing dual-channel fluorescence outputs could be considered as a powerful technique, which can achieve high accuracy and even visual detection. Herein, we have demonstrated for the first time the use of a dual-channel luminescent signal readout nanoprobe o-CDs@methyl red (CDs stands for carbon dots) for rapid, sensitive, and visual detection of biogenic amines. Such probe was prepared by covalently modifying methyl red on NH2-rich carbon dots, in which o-CDs served as a reference signal and methyl red acted as a signal report unit for BAs. Upon the addition of BAs, the fluorescence intensity of methyl red increases, while that of o-CDs remains almost unchanged. It was found that such fluorescence platform can detect tyramine with high sensitivity (LOD: 0.01 μM) and fast response (30 s). More importantly, the proposed method can be successfully applied for biogenic amines detection in real milk and yogurt samples, indicating that the as-prepared probe holds the further potential for practical application in control and monitoring of food quality and freshness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Biogenic amines
Ratiometric sensor
Carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of o-CDs@methyl red. The inset shows the size distribution of the as-obtained o-CDs@methyl red. (b) The FT-IR spectra of o-CDs, methyl red, and o-CDs@methyl red. (c) UV–vis spectra of o-CDs, methyl red, and o-CDs@ methyl red in aqueous solution. (d) The fluorescence emission of o-CDs, methyl red, and o-CDs@methyl red.

(a) Fluorescence emission intensity of o-CDs@methyl red at different concentrations of tyramine from 0 to 5 µM. (b) The linear applicable curve displaying F MR/F CDs versus tyramine concentration, λex = 365 nm. Insert: The color change of o-CDs@methyl red after exposure to tyramine with 365 nm ultraviolet light.
Fluorescence emission intensity of o-CDs@methyl red in different time (0–30 min) upon exposure to tyramine at different concentration from 0 to 5 µM (λex = 365 nm).

Fluorescence emission intensity of o-CDs@methyl red upon titration of (a) milk and (c) yogurt samples with and without tyramine. Concentration dependence of fluorescence intensity ratios of F MR/F CDs in the range of 0–5 μM in milk (b) and yogurt (d) samples.
Process for methyl red (MR) modified o-CDs and concept for sensing biogenic amines.
Recovery Results of Milk and Yogurt Samples Containing Different Concentrations of Tyramine.

head.ce:title: A dual-channel luminescent signal readout nanoprobe for rapid monitoring of biogenic amines in milk and yogurt

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: G.M. and C.Z. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As biologically active small molecular, biogenic amines (BAs) could be served as an important index of food spoilage and contamination. In addition, excessive intake of biogenic amines usually can cause a serious impact on human health. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a reliable, sensitive, and non-invasive method for the detection of biogenic amines in foods. To address the above problems, optical sensing method utilizing dual-channel fluorescence outputs could be considered as a powerful technique, which can achieve high accuracy and even visual detection. Herein, we have demonstrated for the first time the use of a dual-channel luminescent signal readout nanoprobe o-CDs@methyl red (CDs stands for carbon dots) for rapid, sensitive, and visual detection of biogenic amines. Such probe was prepared by covalently modifying methyl red on NH2-rich carbon dots, in which o-CDs served as a reference signal and methyl red acted as a signal report unit for BAs. Upon the addition of BAs, the fluorescence intensity of methyl red increases, while that of o-CDs remains almost unchanged. It was found that such fluorescence platform can detect tyramine with high sensitivity (LOD: 0.01 μM) and fast response (30 s). More importantly, the proposed method can be successfully applied for biogenic amines detection in real milk and yogurt samples, indicating that the as-prepared probe holds the further potential for practical application in control and monitoring of food quality and freshness.
Herein, we reported a dual-signal fluorescent sensing platform for biogenic amine detection in milk and yogurt.

ce:section.ce:para: Biogenic amines (BAs), as nitrogenous organic bases, which are mainly produced by thermal or bacterial enzymatic decarboxylation of corresponding amino acids, are found in a wide variety of common foods and beverages such as cheese, beef, fish, and red wine, etc [1]. Among the BAs, histamine (HI), tyramine (TA), putrescine (PUT), phenylethylamine (PEA), and cadaverine (CAD) are considered to be common biogenic amines in deteriorated foods. BAs are generally harmless at low concentrations, unfortunately, excessive intake of biogenic amines in the spoiled food can cause some physiological symptoms including respiratory distress, headache, hypertension, and vomiting [2–4]. In addition, as well-known indicator of food spoilage and contamination, the level of biogenic amines in these foods can be used for evaluating the quality of food to eliminate consumer confusion and reduce food waste [5,6]. Considering the role in the indication of food spoilage and the various undesirable physiological effect, the accurate and facile monitoring of biogenic amines in related samples is of critical importance and highly valuable, from both economic and consumers’ health perspective.

In the past few decades, numerous analytical methods, such as electrochemical [7,8], chemiresistive [9], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [10] and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [11] have been established for the detection of BAs. Unfortunately, these methods often suffer from various limitations, such as time consuming, labor-intensive, and require expensive equipment, which often severely impede their routine use and further practical applications. In order to overcome above issues, it is significant to develop sensing technology for simple, fast, accurate, and cost-effective detection of BAs, mostly focusing on fluorescence sensing method.

Recently, fluorescent probe technology has been extensively explored for detecting various chemical or biological analytes, such as metal ions [12–16], anions [17–21], VOC [22], mRNA [23], biological small molecules, and proteins [24–30]. The fluorescence sensors may also serve as a promising candidate to monitor BAs [31–34]. Nevertheless, studies on using fluorescence sensors to detect biogenic amines are still very limited and not reached full potential to date. And, most currently reported fluorescent probes for BAs use the sole sensing signals and suffer from a relative long response time, which hinders their further application. Compared with common single fluorescence probes, ratiometric fluorescent sensors possess many advantages, such as minimal anti-interference of the instrument and external environmental background, enabling a more accurate and reliable quantitation [35–37]. Thus, to fulfill the requirements for BAs sensing, without doubt, it is very intriguing and valuable to explore novel fluorescence probe capable of ratiometric response towards BAs. However, the construction of ratiometric fluorescence probes often requires elaborate design.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a fascinating class of carbon nanoparticle, continues to gain tremendous attraction in recent years in the area of fluorescent nanomaterials due to their excellent properties, for instance, facile preparation, bright fluorescence, low toxicity, cost-effective, and excellent chemical stability [38–41]. Recently, Hu et. al reported a carbon-dots-based sensor for dynamical and reversible detection of hypochlorite and ascorbic acid (AA) in living cells [42]. Chen and coworkers reported a dual-emissive carbon dots for nitrite determination in food samples [43]. It will be desirable to combine carbon dots with other light-emitting material to design hybrid or functionalized fluorescent probes for applications. Zhang and co-coworkers reported an ionic-liquid-stabilized fluorescent probe based on S-doped carbon dot-embedded covalent-organic frameworks for determination of histamine [44]. Wang et.al prepared molecularly imprinted polymers based on carbon dots-embedded covalent-organic frameworks for efficiently detecting tryptamine in meat samples [45]. Although many luminescence sensors have been investigated so far, probes based on CDs@dye exhibiting turn-on and ratiometric sensing behavior toward BAs are still very rare, to our knowledge.

In this work, a dual-channel luminescent signal readout nanoprobe for rapid, facile, and sensitive detection of BAs was successfully developed ( Scheme 1). The nanoprobe was prepared by the incorporation of pH indicators methyl red and carbon dots into inorganic materials based on amidation of carboxyl groups on CDs and amino groups on MR. In such nanoprobe, o-CDs served as the internal reference, whereas methyl red as the fluorescence response signal of biogenic amines based on methyl red as a weak basic acid-base indicator. The o-CDs@methyl red were employed as a probe for determination of tyramine (TA) with a detection limit as 0.01 μM. In particular, the sensor enabled visual fluorescence detection of tyramine in solution. Finally, the nanoprobe we proposed was successfully employed as a quick-responsive, low cost, and high sensitivity sensing platform for monitoring the BAs in real milk and yogurt samples, which has immense potential for application in food quality monitor.

Methyl red (MR) and tyramide (TA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Tryptamine and dimethylamine were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. 2-Ethoxy-1-ethoxycarbonyl-1, 2-dihydroquinoline (EEDQ) was received from Shanghai Dibai Chemicals. The milk and yogurt were bought from supermarket.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were taken using a Talos F200s. FT-IR characterization was recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were achieved by a UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were measured by using a Shimadzu RF-5301 fluorophotometer.

First, one-pot solvothermal method was applied to synthesize the green emissive CDs (o-CDs) according to a reported literature [46]. First, 0.2 g of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in ethanol (20 mL), and the resulting solution was transferred to a high-pressure Teflon autoclave and heated in oven at 180 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained orange suspensions were purified with a silica column chromatography using mixtures of methanol and methylene chloride as eluents. The yield of purified o-CDs was around 12%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for synthesis of o-CDs@methyl red
ce:section.ce:para: For preparing the dual-emission fluorescence probe o-CDs@methyl red, 10 mg of methyl red was dissolved in the N-ethoxycarbonyl-2-ethoxy-1,2,-dihydroquinoline (EEDQ) ethanol solution (3 mg/mL) and stirred for 30 min at room temperature to activate the carboxylate groups of methyl red. Then, 2 mL of NH2-rich carbon dots (o-CDs) was added to the activated methyl red solution, and the mixture was stirred vigorously under darkness for 12 h. The product was collected by removing the excess unreacted chemicals by the centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe was collected for characterization and further use.

In a typical experiment, 2 µL of o-CDs@MR was added into double ultrapure water (2 mL). Then, different concentrations of biogenic amine were added to the o-CDs@MR solution, and allowed to stand for 30 s before detection. The emission spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer upon 365 nm excitation. For selectivity experiments, possible interference amino acids (L-phenylalanine, L-threonine, L-methionine, and L-serine) were added, the same assay procedure was conducted.

To confirm the successful fabrication of o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe and further investigate the properties, the obtained o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe developed in this work has been characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (IR), UV absorption spectra, and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1a, o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe displayed a good monodispersity. The shape and size of o-CDs were not significantly affected in o-CDs@ methyl red composites compared with alone o-CDs (Fig. S1). Moreover, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were used to explore the possible chemical bonds and functional groups in o-CDs, methyl red, and o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe. As seen in Fig. 1b, the FTIR spectrum of o-CDs shows a characteristic broad band between 3100－3500 cm−1, manifesting the existence of oxygen and amino containing functional groups on its surfaces. In the spectrum of methyl red, two bands observed at 1603 and 1369 cm−1 are assigned to the νasym (NN) and νsym (NN) bonds in azo dyes. The peaks at 1715 and 1148 cm−1, related to the CO and C–O carbonyl stretching vibration, respectively. As for the o-CDs@methyl red nanohybrid, some similar peaks of o-CDs and methyl red were exhibited. In addition, a distinct new peak associated with the C-N stretching vibration at 1065 cm−1 appeared.

The ability of methyl red to attach onto the surface of the o-CDs was also confirmed by UV absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. It can be observed that the UV spectrum of o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe emerged two principal absorption peaks centered at 435 nm and 552 nm, which consistent with the absorption bands of o-CDs and methyl red (Fig. 1c). The fluorescence spectra of o-CDs, methyl red, and o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe are shown in Fig. 1d. The o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe exhibited two emission peaks at 420 and 530 nm, which corresponds to the emission spectra of methyl red and o-CDs, respectively. Subsequently, the emission wavelengths of nanoprobe at different excitation wavelengths from 345 to 380 nm were preliminarily discussed. For o-CDs@methyl red, in order to excite both o-CDs and methyl red at a single wavelength, 365 nm was selected as excitation wavelength in the following experiments (Fig. S2).

Furthermore, to testify the fluorescence stability of o-CD and o-CDs@methyl red, the corresponding measurements were performed. Only slight fluorescence intensity change was found even after 1 h of continuous UV illumination (365 nm), suggesting the excellent anti-photobleaching capability of carbon dots (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensities of o-CDs@methyl red in water retain stability even after three days at ambient temperature, which established the solid foundation for the forthcoming research and practical application (Fig. S4).

Among biogenic amines, tyramine as a naturally occurring monoamine produced by decarboxylation of the microbial tyrosine, is considered to be common biogenic amines in deteriorated foods [47]. Foodstuffs containing considerable amounts of tyramine often include fermented food, dairy products, etc. Tyramine is associated with different symptoms such as hives, water retention and asthma, and the most common side effect is migraine. Thus, tyramine was chosen as a representative to conduct fluorescence sensing study toward biogenic amines in this study.

First, o-CDs@methyl red was added into double ultrapure water. Then, different concentrations of tyramine were added to the o-CDs@methyl red dispersion and reacted for 30 s. As designed, we found that when tyramine with the concentration from 0 μM to 5 μM was added to the aqueous solution of the o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe, the emission intensity of methyl red in the probe increases gradually with the concentration increase of tyramine, while that of o-CDs almost remains constant ( Fig. 2a). This trend of fluorescence change is consistent with the response of single methyl red or o-CDs to tyramine (Fig. S6). A good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.993) was found between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F MR/F CDs) and tyramine concentration between 0 and 5 μM, with fitting equation of F MR/F CDs =0.65[TA] + 1.07, indicating that o-CDs@methyl red has great potential for quantitative determination of BAs (Fig. 2b). Moreover, the detection limit of the developed probe toward tyramine was estimated to be 0.01 μM (S/N = 3), which is compare to or better than many previously reported methods for BA sensing (Table S1). It’s worth noting that the addition of tyramine to the solutions of o-CDs@methyl red resulted in an obvious color change from orange to blue under UV light irradiation, which can be easily visualized by the naked eye (Fig. 2b, insert).

A series of potentially coexisting substances were examined to assess the selectivity of the assembled sensing platform for tyramine, including L-phenylalanine, L-threonine, L-methionine, and L-serine. As depicted in Fig. S7, the coexistence of these substances had negligible interference on the tyramine recognition. Considering that a number of volatile components are emitted during food spoilage including alcohol, the fluorescence response of o-CDs@methyl red toward ethanol was also evaluated. Fig. S8 shows that there are no obvious response for ethanol. Good specificity allows the probe o-CDs@methyl red to be considered for tyramine monitor in real food sample.

Subsequently, the time response of the probe to tyramine detection in aqueous solution was studied. As shown in Fig. 3, the fluorescence response of o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe to tyramine is very fast. The process takes only 30 s to reach equilibrium, even the tyramine level was as low as 1 µM. In fact, o-CDs@methyl red nanoprobe is able to detect tyramine more rapidly than many other methods previously reported. In addition, the probe can be obtained by simple preparation and cheap raw materials, unlike some other materials prepared with a time-consuming or complicated procedure.

After detailed study on tyramine sensing, the fluorescence responses of probe toward tryptamine and dimethylamine were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S9 and S10, the fluorescence changes of nanoprobe toward tryptamine and dimethylamine are similar to that of tyramine. The tryptamine (TRY) concentration showed a good linear correlation with the fluorescence intensity ratio (F MR/F CDs) in the range of 0 − 200 μM, the corresponding linear regression equation was F MR/F CDs = 0.008[TRY] + 1.098, R 2 = 0.9987 (Fig. S9). For dimethylamine (DMA), a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9985) was found between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F MR/F CDs) and the concentration of dimethylamine between 0 and 65 μM, with fitting equation of F MR/F CDs = 0.0187[DMA]+ 1.438 (Fig. S10).

The probe o-CDs@methyl red can monitor biological amines mainly based on the fact that biogenic amines are a weak base substance, and methyl red is a good acid-base indicator for biogenic amines. Methyl red is a weakly basic azo dye that has two forms of monoprotonated and deprotonated [48]. After forming o-CDs@methyl red, covalent amide linkage between the amine group of o-CDs and the free carboxylic acid group of methyl red removed the acidic proton of methyl red. In this case, the carboxylic acid group of the methyl red is no longer available to participate in the structural changes, and the halogen behavior of o-CDs@methyl red will result in a change in the fluorescent signal of the probe [49]. The mechanism is illustrated in the Scheme 2.

In order to demonstrate the potential application of o-CDs@methyl red probes as an optical sensor in actual food sample detection, the proposed sensor was used to determine tryptamine contents in milk and yogurt samples. Milk and yogurt have been processed using previously reported method [50], in which 10 g of milk or yogurt sample added to 6 mL 5% perchloric acid with magnetic stirring for 30 min and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min several times for protein coagulation. The precipitation was washed with 4 mL 5% perchloric acid and centrifuged many times until no precipitation was produced. The centrifuged supernatant was collected and concentrated to 10 mL. To estimate the suitability of the sensing platform in actual samples, method of standard addition was conducted. For each test, 50 μL of milk or yogurt samples were mixed with different known concentrations of tyramine. As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescence signals of nanoprobes in water, milk and yogurt are almost the same. The results suggested that there was no tyramine or the content of tyramine is below the detection limit of the probe in real samples. Subsequently, these real milk and yogurt samples were spiked with different known concentration of tyramine. As can be seen from Table 1, the satisfactory recoveries range from 82.5% to 105.6%, and the relatively low standard deviation (RSD) of each sample was achieved, indicating that the proposed probe for monitoring BAs in real samples was trustworthy and practicable. o-CDs@methyl red was also used to monitor the time-dependent spoilage of real milk samples. The corresponding fluorescence photographs were collected, as shown in Fig. S11.

In summary, a dual-channel luminescent signal readout nanoprobe, o-CDs@methyl red, was successfully constructed for nondestructive, rapid, ultra-sensitive, and visual detection of biogenic amines. In this elaborated detection platform, the o-CDs just acted as reference signal and a pH-responsive methyl red served as a reporting unit for biogenic amines. The carboxylic group of the acid-base indicator of methyl red is easily modified, which can form a covalent amide bond with the amino group of the carbon quantum dot. Compared with pure methyl red, after the amide reaction, the water solubility of the as-obtained o-CDs@methyl red composite was improved. Overall, our strategy of using o-CDs@ methyl red luminescent sensor for detecting BAs shows several advantages in the aspects of (1) dual-channel luminescent sensor greatly improves the reliability of the detection result; (2) The detection process is more rapidly than many other methods previously reported, and only takes 30 s; (3) The developed probe is able to analyze tyramine with a low detection limit of 0.01 μM; and (4) fluorescence color change could be recognized by the naked eye. Furthermore, the feasibility of the proposed fluorescence probe has been proven by the determination of tyramine in actual samples, including milk and yogurt sample. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that using dual-channel luminescent signal output strategy based on CDs@dye for sensitive and visual monitoring of biogenic amines, which could be used for the food spoilage detecting application, especially for intelligent packaging.

Genping Meng: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Caizhong Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Peiyao Du: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shihao Sun: Methodology. Xuan Zhang: Methodology. Baodui Wang: Visualization, Supervision. Xiaoquan Lu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22001193, 22127803 and 22174110); the Industrial Support Plan of Gansu Provincial Department of Education (2021cyzc-01); Special Fund Project for Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development by the Central Government (No. 2020-2060503-17); the Program for Chang Jiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team, Ministry of Education, China (IRT-16R61); the Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurial for Talent, Lanzhou, Gansu Province, China (2014-RC-39).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131435.

Supplementary material

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Significance of biogenic amines to food safety and human health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.R. Shalaby, Significance of biogenic amines to food safety and human health, Food Res. Int. 29 (1996) 675–690.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The problem of biogenic amines in fermented foods and the use of potential biogenic amine-degrading microorganisms as a solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Alvarez, M.V. Moreno-Arribas, The problem of biogenic amines in fermented foods and the use of potential biogenic amine-degrading microorganisms as a solution, Trends Food Sci. Technol. 39 (2014) 146–155.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J.A. Pereira, P. Porto-Figueira, B. Andrade, P. Gonçalves, J. Pataca, J.S. Câmara, Biogenic amines in food: Occurrence and analytical challenges for their analysis, Biog. Amines 1 2017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High affinity aptamer for the detection of the biogenic amine histamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Mairal Lerga, M. Jauset-Rubio, V. Skouridou, A.S. Bashammakh, M.S. El-Shahawi, A.O. Alyoubi, C.K. O’Sullivan, High Affinity Aptamer for the Detection of the Biogenic Amine Histamine, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 7104–7111.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impact of biogenic amines on food quality and safety

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ruiz-Capillas, A.M. Herrero, Impact of Biogenic Amines on Food Quality and Safety, Foods 8 (2019) 62.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biogenic amines content as a measure of the quality of wines of Abruzzo (Italy)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Martuscelli, G. Arfelli, A.C. Manetta, G. Suzzi, Biogenic amines content as a measure of the quality of wines of Abruzzo (Italy), Food Chem. 140 (2013) 590–597.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of tyramine with ITO/APTES/ErGO electrode and its application in real sample analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Khan, X. Liu, J. Zhu, F. Ma, W. Hu, X. Liu, Electrochemical Detection of Tyramine with ITO/APTES/ErGO Electrode and its Application in Real Sample Analysis, Biosens. Bioelectron. 108 (2018) 76–81.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of histamine in fish sauce using heterogeneous carbon electrodes modified with rhenium(Ⅳ) oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Veseli, M. Vasjari, T. Arbneshi, A. Hajrizi, Ľ. Švorc, A. Samphao, K. Kalcher, Electrochemical determination of histamine in fish sauce using heterogeneous carbon electrodes modified with rhenium(Ⅳ) oxide, Sens. Actuators, B 228 (2016) 774–781.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-walled carbon nanotube/metalloporphyrin composites for the chemiresistive detection of amines and meat spoilage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.F. Liu, A.R. Petty, G.T. Sazama, T.M. Swager, Single-walled carbon nanotube/metalloporphyrin composites for the chemiresistive detection of amines and meat spoilage, Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 54 (2015) 6554–6557.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A multi-purpose tool for food inspection: Simultaneous determination of various classes of preservatives and biogenic amines in meat and fish products by LC-MS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Molognoni, H. Daguer, L.A. de Sá Ploêncio, J.D. D. Lindner, A multi-purpose tool for food inspection: Simultaneous determination of various classes of preservatives and biogenic amines in meat and fish products by LC-MS, Talanta 178 (2018) 1053–1066.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct solid phase microextraction combined with gas chromatography-Mass spectrometry for the determination of biogenic amines in wine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhang, Y. Wang, W. Geng, H. Liu, Rapid detection of tryptamine by optosensor with molecularly imprinted polymers based on carbon dots-embedded covalent-organic frameworks, Sens. Actuators, B 285 (2019) 546–552.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Sheng, C. Sun, G. Fang, X. Wu, G. Hu, Y. Zhang, S. Wang, Development of an Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay for the Detection of Tyramine as an Index of Freshness in Meat and Seafood, J. Agric. Food Chem. 64 (2016) 8944–8949.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Tawarah, H.M. Abu-Shamleh, A spectrophotometric study of the acid-base equilibria of o-methyl red in aqueous solutions, Dyes Pigm. 17 (1991) 203–215.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent center for monitoring of food spoilage based on dye covalently modified eumofs: from sensory hydrogels to logic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-Y. Xu, X. Lian, J.-N. Hao, C. Zhang, B. Yan, A. Double-Stimuli-Responsive Fluorescent Center for Monitoring of Food Spoilage based on Dye Covalently Modified EuMOFs: From Sensory Hydrogels to Logic Devices, Adv. Mater. 29 (2017) 1702298.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembled conjugated polymer/chitosan-graft-oleic acid micelles for fast visible detection of aliphatic biogenic amines by a “turn-on” FRET

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhong, C. Liu, W. Ge, R. Sun, F. Huang, X. Wang, Self-assembled conjugated polymer/chitosan-graft-oleic acid micelles for fast visible detection of aliphatic biogenic amines by a “turn-on” FRET, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 22875–22884.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Genping Meng is currently a PhD candidate at Lanzhou University, China. His research interests include the development and application of carbon dots based materials.
Caizhong Zhang received a master’s degree from the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Northwest Normal University, China. Her research focus is the development of fluorescence sensors.
Peiyao Du is a member of Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu. Her main research interests are the development and application of sensors.
Shihao Sun is currently a PhD candidate at Lanzhou University, China. His research interests include material science.
Xuan Zhang is currently a postgraduate at Lanzhou University, China. Her research interests include functional nanoprobes.

Baodui Wang is a professor of chemistry at Lanzhou University, China. He received his PhD in inorganic chemistry from Lanzhou University. His current research interests include bioinorganic chemistry, optical sensors and material science.

Xiaoquan Lu is the director of Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu, China. He was awarded “Yangtze Scholars Program” of ministry of education, distinguished professor in 2013. His research interest focuses on electroanalytical chemistry, biosensors, etc.
